turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the Court in Case 23-1300, Nuclear Regulatory Commission v. Texas and the consolidated case.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAVANAUGH
turns.text: In 1954, Congress passed and President Eisenhower signed the Atomic Energy Act. That Act allowed for private commercial nuclear power plants. Today, more than 50 nuclear power plants, along with coal, natural gas, and renewable energy sources, produce electricity for American homes and businesses. In all, nuclear power plants generate almost 20% of the electricity in America. Nuclear power plants also create dangerous radioactive waste, as relevant here, spent nuclear fuel. Many nuclear power plants store spent nuclear fuel on-site. Because some plants are shutting down or no longer operating, on-site storage is not a viable long-term solution. To address the storage problem, federal law has long designated the Yucca Mountain nuclear waste repository in Nevada as the future permanent site for disposal of spent nuclear fuel. But the Nevada project has caused significant political controversy, to put it mildly, and has stalled. To fill the void, some private businesses have sought to build and operate facilities to store spent nuclear fuel off-site, that is, off the site of a nuclear power plant. To do so, however, they need to obtain licenses from the Nuclear Regulatory Commission, a government agency that reviews license applications for nuclear power plants and storage facilities. In 2018, Interim Storage Partners, a business known as ISP, applied to the Commission for a license to build a private off-site storage facility in Andrews County, Texas. Andrews County is in West Texas, northwest of Midland, north of I-20, adjacent to the Texas-New Mexico border. Several entities sought to intervene in the licensing proceeding, including Fasken, a business that grazes cattle and operates oil and gas wells in West Texas. Fasken objected to the proposed facility and raised various concerns, including possible environmental contamination and harm to endangered species. The Commission denied Fasken's petition to intervene. The Commission determined that Fasken did not satisfy the requirements for intervention under the Commission's intervention regulations. Fasken then sought review in the D.C. Circuit, challenging the commission's denial of intervention, but the D.C. Circuit decided that Fasken was not entitled to intervene. Fasken did not then petition for review in this court on the issue of the denial of intervention. So the D.C. Circuit's decision upholding the denial of Fasken's intervention petition is final, and that is important as I will explain. Meanwhile, as part of the licensing proceeding that was ongoing, the Commission issued a draft environmental statement, or EIS, as required by the National Environmental Policy Act. A Texas government agency and Fasken both commented to the Commission on the draft EIS. But in September 2021, the Commission made its final decision, granting ISP a license to build and operate its proposed off-site facility for storage of spent nuclear fuel. Texas and Fasken then sued in the Fifth Circuit arguing that the Commission lacked statutory authority to license storage of spent nuclear fuel at a private off-site facility as distinct from on-site facilities. The threshold question here is whether Texas and Fasken may maintain this suit. The Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals said, "yes, we disagree." Under the Hobbs Act, which is the law that provides for judicial review of Nuclear Regulatory Commission licensing decisions, only an aggrieved party may obtain judicial review of a commission licensing decision. The key statutory word is "party", and to qualify as a party to a licensing proceeding, the Atomic Energy Act requires, as relevant here, that one have successfully intervened in the licensing proceeding. It is not enough to merely try to intervene or to comment on an environmental impact statement, for example. One must successfully intervene. In this case; however, Texas and Fasken did not successfully intervene in the licensing proceedings before the Commission. Fasken tried to intervene, but was denied, and as noted, that decision was upheld by the D.C. Circuit. Fasken cannot now relitigate that denial of intervention in this Hobbs Act proceeding for review of the licensing decision. And Texas never even tried to intervene in the Commission's licensing proceeding, because neither Fasken nor Texas successfully intervened in the licensing proceeding, neither was a party eligible to obtain judicial review in the Fifth Circuit. For that reason, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals and do not decide the underlying statutory dispute over whether the Nuclear Regulatory Commission possesses authority to license private off-site storage facilities. Justice Gorsuch has filed the dissenting opinion which Justice Thomas and Justice Alito have joined.
